agency_and_respondent_information.name_o.answer: Monterey County Department of Social Services, Family & Children’s Services

agency_and_respondent_information.positi.answer: Deputy Director, Family & Children’s Services Division

a_background_of_person_completing_form.a.answer:
I currently serve as the Deputy Director for the Family & Children’s Services Division of the Monterey County Department of Social Services. I stepped into this role in March 2020. Prior to that, I held several positions within the same division:

- 2011–2014: Emergency Response Social Worker (Child Protective Services). I carried a mixed caseload focusing on initial investigations, with a significant proportion of referrals involving Black and Latino families in East Salinas and Seaside.
- 2014–2017: Senior Social Worker / Lead for Differential Response. In this role, I supervised a small team piloting differential response strategies and began informally tracking racial and ethnic patterns in referrals and outcomes.
- 2017–2020: Program Manager, Permanency & Family Engagement Unit. I oversaw Family Finding, concurrent planning, and kinship support services. I also co-chaired our internal Equity and Inclusion Workgroup, which was a direct outgrowth of earlier REI and disproportionality work.

As Deputy Director, my responsibilities include overall management of CPS programs (Emergency Response, Continuing Services, Permanency, Resource Family Approval), oversight of data and quality improvement efforts, and serving as the executive sponsor for our current Racial Equity and Inclusion (REI) strategic plan. I participate in countywide leadership meetings, budget planning, union consultations, and community advisory boards. I also represent the department in regional collaboratives focused on reducing racial disparities in child welfare.

Throughout my time with the agency, a consistent part of my role—formal or informal—has been to question how our policies and practices impact families of color, particularly Black and Indigenous families, and to advocate for changes in screening, removal, and reunification practices that have historically produced worse outcomes for these communities.

a_background_of_person_completing_form.p.answer:
My involvement in the anti-racism and REI-related projects has evolved over time:

1. Family to Family (Annie E. Casey) – 2012–2015
   - Initially, I participated as a frontline social worker on the Team Decision Making (TDM) workgroup. My role was to bring real case examples to the table and identify how TDMs were or were not providing meaningful voice and choice to families, particularly families of color.
   - I helped pilot a practice of tracking who spoke during TDM meetings (e.g., social worker vs. parent vs. extended family) and we found that white professionals dominated the conversation even when Black and Latino parents were present. I shared these findings with our steering committee and helped develop a facilitation checklist that required facilitators to explicitly invite parents and youth to speak first.
   - I also volunteered to serve as a liaison with two community-based organizations in East Salinas (a Latino-serving family resource center and a grassroots youth organization) to improve community representation at TDMs and community partnership meetings.

2. California Disproportionality Project (CDP) – 2015–2018
   - During CDP, I was a Senior Social Worker and then Program Manager. I served on the county’s CDP Core Team. My role included coordinating data pulls with our Quality Assurance unit, facilitating focus groups with Black parents who had prior child welfare involvement, and co-presenting our findings to both line staff and leadership.
   - I co-led an internal workgroup that reviewed our Structured Decision Making (SDM) implementation. We examined how risk and safety assessment tools were being applied and whether they were being used more punitively with Black and Indigenous families. I personally reviewed case files, shadowed staff, and compared cases with similar risk factors but different racial backgrounds. In one example, a Black mother with a substance use issue received an immediate removal, while a white mother with similar history and risk factors was offered in-home services. We used these concrete examples to push for more structured oversight and case consultation.
   - I helped design and facilitate a series of mandatory training sessions titled “Understanding Disproportionality in Our Own Data,” where we walked staff through county-specific charts showing overrepresentation of Black children in foster care compared to their representation in the general child population. I shared case stories from my own caseload and invited parents (who consented and were compensated) to share their experiences.

3. Other Anti-Racism / REI Initiatives – 2018–Present
   - After CDP formally ended, I co-chaired the Equity and Inclusion Workgroup, which was tasked with sustaining the work. We developed an internal racial equity statement, drafted recommendations for changes to our removal decision-making process, and advocated for the creation of an Equity Analyst position (which was funded in 2021).
   - As Deputy Director, I now serve as the executive sponsor for our current REI strategic plan. I oversee the REI Steering Committee, make sure REI goals are built into performance measures, and work with HR on recruitment and promotion strategies to diversify leadership. I also review serious incident reports and appeals with an explicit lens on whether race or implicit bias may have played a role in decision-making.
   - On a very practical level, I regularly participate in case staffing meetings when staff raise concerns about racial dynamics. For example, I’ve joined consultations where staff were unsure whether they were overreacting to parenting practices that were culturally normative for the family’s community. I help staff step back, consider historical context, and differentiate between actual safety threats and discomfort with unfamiliar cultural norms.

Overall, my project role has ranged from frontline participant and data gatherer to facilitator, trainer, and now executive-level champion ensuring the work remains a priority even when there are competing demands and crises.
